headline_assessment: The 2024 price controversy is a boundary-control case: official festival vendors and nearby/night-market vendors may be operationally distinct, but visitors experience both as part of the festival.
confidence: B+ for public apology and official-vendor/night-market distinction; C for exact night-market contract path until agreement documents are found.

short_answer.why_did_it_fail: A night-market food-price controversy damaged the festival experience. The foundation acknowledged management negligence and said it requested correction from the night-market consignment/operator side, while also distinguishing official in-festival vendors selected by foundation bidding and pre-agreed menus/prices.
short_answer.was_it_a_subcontractor_problem: Partly possible but not proven. Public reporting identifies a night-market entrusted/operator side, but the actual agreement, authority scope and price-control obligations are not yet in the dataset.
short_answer.what_must_be_checked_next: Night-market operating agreement, official vendor bid documents, menu/price pre-approval records, correction request record, booth/vendor list and complaint log.

known_facts.fact: The reported disputed purchase was 20,000 won sundae, 7,000 won tteokbokki and 7,000 won noodles, total 34,000 won.
Hongcheon Cultural Foundation apologized for negligent management of night-market stores and said it would monitor prices.
The foundation said official festival vendors inside the venue were selected by foundation bidding and menu/prices were pre-agreed, mostly under 10,000 won.
The foundation said it requested correction from the night-market consignment/operator side and set up a price-gouging report center.
Gangwon tourism information lists the 2025 festival host as Hongcheon County and supervisor/organizer as Hongcheon Cultural Foundation.
Current official festival information publishes core activity ticket prices and local gift-certificate return structures.

why_chain.label: Visitor-facing symptom
why_chain.finding: Food price and portion complaints spread from an online community and created a public dissatisfaction signal.

why_chain.label: Operational boundary
why_chain.finding: Public reporting separates foundation-selected in-venue vendors from nearby/night-market stores.

why_chain.label: Control failure
why_chain.finding: Even if outside the official vendor package, night-market sellers were close enough to damage festival trust, so the brand-risk control boundary was wider than the contract boundary.

why_chain.label: Corrective response
why_chain.finding: The foundation acknowledged negligence, requested price correction and promised monitoring/report-center operation.

why_chain.label: Proof gap
why_chain.finding: The dataset still lacks the night-market consignment agreement, official vendor bid package, final vendor list and inspection results.

comparison_to_yeongyang.same_pattern: Both cases show that vendors perceived as part of the festival can damage the whole festival brand even when the organizer frames them as external or boundary cases.
comparison_to_yeongyang.different_pattern: Yeongyang has a stronger external-mobile-merchant responsibility admission and broker/seat-fee lead; Hongcheon has a clearer official-vendor versus night-market boundary and a foundation bidding/pre-agreed price statement.
comparison_to_yeongyang.research_priority: Hongcheon should be used to map formal vendor boundaries and price-control obligations; Yeongyang should be used to map external merchant and seat-fee pressure.

evidence_gaps: Night-market consignment/operator agreement and price-control clause
Official festival vendor bid notice, award result and menu/price approval records
Whether night-market stores were inside, adjacent to or branded by the festival layout
Price-gouging report-center complaint log and correction result
2025/2026 recurrence or prevention evidence, including menu-board publication
